A NEW PRIVATE PRESS.

It is highly gratifying to know that wnere are some scholarly gen-
tlemen still remaining, who, imbued with a love of the printer’s art
and conscious of the power of the press, are sufficiently public-spirited
to establish and maintam a i)rinmting plant at their residences. We
had feared that this pleasant and exceedingly wuseful pastime had
quite died out in Ireland. Im the past—thanks t¢ the able assistanoe
of many contributors—we have placed on record in these pages all
avallable details of almost all the private presses that were ever active
in Jreland. Now we record with pleasure that there is yet one in ex-
istence at the Rectory, Charlestown, near Ardee, in Co. Louth, worked
by the Rev. Guy W. C. L’Estrange, M.A., Rector of that parish, and
to that gentleman we are indebted for a handsome specimen of his
handicraft and literary ability. It is entitled ‘‘Notes and Jottings
concerning tho Parish of Charlestown Union. . . . Charlestown:
Printed at the Rectory Press, 1912.”” It measures 8% by 53, and runs
to viii. + 94 pp., containing 14 full page illustrations firom photographs
mainly taken by the author and printed off by him. The workmanship
reaches & high level, and the book is valuable not only as a choice
specimen of Irish private printing, but as am admirable addition to
Irish topographical and genealogiocal hiterature, comtaining, as it does,
much interesting local and family history, printed here for the first
time. The book is intended for presentation to the parishioners ¢ as

I am pleased to hear that University College, Cork, has obtained
by purchase the famous Celtic Library form ed by the late Prof.
D’Arbois de Jubainville, which embraces the entire range of Celtic
study. The purchase was recom mended by Prof. Kuno Meyer and Mr.
R. I. Best, the latter of whom it will be remembered translated ‘‘ The
Irish Mythological Cycle ’ from the French of Prof. D’Arbois some ten

In the series of History Lectures ‘‘ Lords-Deputies and Viceroys of
Jreland, 1174-1692,”” a paper on * Sir Arthur Chichester, Lord-Deputy
1606-1616,”” was read by Miss Janet Perry, B.A. In the course of her
paper the lecturer pointed out that, in some matters, the foundations
of modern Ireland were laid during Chichester’s Deputyship. Witk
the Flight of the Earls the last Irish stronghold was lost; and as a
result of the shiring and plantation of Ulster, carried out under
Chichester, ‘‘unconquered Ulster”’ became the least Irish part of
Ireland. Chichester cannot be accused of treachery towards the Earls
nor of causing their flicht, which took him by surprise; his duplicity
in behaving with the utmost friendliness to O’Neill at a time when he
was taking down all St. Lawrence’s depositions against him was
merely his conception of diplomacy as it should be practised by all
sagacious Deputies. The Plantation of Ulster would have been car-
ried out with far more justice to the Irish and with far greater per-
manent advantage to I'ngland if Chichester’s scheme for it had been
adopted. IHe intended that the Irish inbabitants should have first
consideration, and that the undertakers should be made to fulfi
strictly all the conditions under which they got their grants. The
English Council overruled Chichester, with the results that the Trish,
who formed three-quarters of the population ‘of Ulster, got one-tenth
of the land—and that the worst part—and that by 1617 much of the
territory had lapsed, through the indolence of the undertakers, into
Irish hands again. A zealous Protestant, Chichester declared that

“ your wicked religion was the cause of the late rebellion’” (Hugh

0'Neill’s), and tried to pack his Parliament of 1613 by creating 39
new boroughs, chiefly in the Protestant districts, with the object of
passing new recusancy laws. But his zeal never made him impolitic.
Finding the Catheolic Opposition party in Parliament to be strong and:
resolute, he yielded gracefully, and making a virtue of necessity, wrote
to England ‘I have dealt as tenderly with men’s consciences as might.
be.”” The permanent division of Ireland into shires was Chichester’s
most creditable work as Deputy. There he achieved, in fact, what
Sussex, Sidney and Perrot had only done on paper, and ensured the:
complete subjugation of the Irish clans. His least creditable work was
his attempt to drive the Irish language out of Ireland. But for the-
successful labours of the Four Masters, Keating and Colgan, in the-
years immediately subsequent to Chichester’s Deputyship, Irish liter-
ature and scholarship might have come to an end in the seventeenth
century. Withal Chichester was an honest and zealous man, a wiser
ruler of Ireland than most of his predecessors, even though misguided
—as all of them were hound to be from the ciroumstances of their
office. ' _

Lorp AsuBouRNE. A good deal of curiosity has arisen as to whethep
the new Lord Ashbourne will take his seat in the House of Lords, and
if he decides to do so, whether he will appear in the ancient Irish
national garb in which, as the Hon. Wm. Gibson, he was habitually at-
tired. He is hardly likely to give up his kilt for the sake of conven-
tionality. He always wears the saffron-coloured kilts in London and
Dorking, where he has a country seat. Lord Ashbourne speaks Irish
fluently, and has been a frequent contributor to magazines and reviews.
—*¢ Daily News.”’

CanoN HanNay holds a place of his own in the public life of Ireland.
He is a Protestant Clergyman, born and reared in Ulster, but at the
same time an ardent Home Ruler and a still more ardent Gaelic
Leaguer. A man of much charm and sensibility, alert, tolerant, cul-
tivated, and practical, he has none of the prejudices either of his class
or his profession. When he speaks he speaks out, with a sincerity ab
least as great as his humour. He has plenty of idealism and a sus-
tained power of enthusiasm, but very few illusions, and none whatever
about Ireland or the Irish. He knows the country through and
through ; not a single stitch in the many seams of Irish life and poli-
tics has escapel his cool, clear gaze. Yet he never despairs either of
the land or its people. Beuteath the caustic and unfaltering writer
there is the worker and the patriot, hopeful, indefatigable, and undis-
mayed.—*‘ Daily Chronicle.” '

Ii. D. J. WiLson. What distinguishes the Irish journalist from his
brethren is, I think, his extraordinany capacity for turning out copy,
and often surprisingly gdod copy. This faculty Wilson possessed in
tho highest degree when he was at his prime, and his writings were to
be found all over the place, though his name seldom appeared save in
monthly reviews. In his latter days, long after he had ceased to look
at books himself, it soothed him to hear his devoted wife read aloud
some of the poems and stories he had loved.—'* British Weekly.”
